title: Product and Platform Ideas That Could Shift Attention Incentives
thesis: Shifting attention incentives from extraction to substance will require a portfolio of product, platform, and protocol changes—quality-weighted ranking, public-interest switches, portable reputation, user-owned attention budgets, and agent-mediated curation—each with distinct build-new, change-existing, or protocol-layer paths and each exposed to gaming, capture, and cultural-transfer risks.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: A platform that asks whether content leaves the user more capable, informed, or connected would need different signals, ranking objectives, and business models than one that asks only whether it kept the user watching.

evidence.snippet: Mozilla Foundation research documented cases in which engagement-based recommendations pushed users toward harmful or misleading content, suggesting that ranking objectives can have public consequences.
The Center for Humane Technology’s Time Well Spent framing argues that technology should align with user goals and respect attention as a finite resource.

counterevidence.summary: Some engagement signals do correlate with genuine satisfaction, and platforms argue that watch time and clicks reflect revealed user preference rather than pure extraction.

claims.claim: Public-interest algorithms could surface locally relevant, verified, and constructive content when the stakes are high.

evidence.snippet: Mozilla Foundation research documented cases in which engagement-based recommendations pushed users toward harmful or misleading content, suggesting that ranking objectives can have public consequences.
Community moderation on Stack Exchange elevates answers that are accurate and well-explained, demonstrating that ranking can be aligned with collective usefulness.

counterevidence.summary: Defining public interest requires contested value judgments and accountable governance; without transparency, public-interest algorithms can be captured by political or commercial interests.

claims.claim: Creator reputation economies can reward consistent quality over viral moments.

evidence.snippet: Stack Exchange reputation accumulates across questions and answers, rewarding sustained helpful participation rather than single viral posts.
Research on Stack Overflow’s reputation economy shows that users with higher reputation tend to produce more consistently valued content over time.

counterevidence.summary: Reputation systems can ossify into status hierarchies, incentivize performative helpfulness, and be manipulated by coordinated voting or purchased endorsements.

claims.claim: User-owned attention budgets and time-well-spent dashboards can restore agency without removing choice.

evidence.snippet: The Center for Humane Technology’s Time Well Spent framing argues that technology should align with user goals and respect attention as a finite resource.
Research on recommendation harms highlights that users often lack meaningful controls over what algorithms show them next.

counterevidence.summary: Dashboards and limits alone have mixed effects on behavior; without friction and defaults, users may ignore self-set boundaries.

claims.claim: Build-new platforms can optimize for substance from inception but face cold-start and network-effects barriers; change-existing strategies have distribution but face incumbent incentive resistance; protocol layers can avoid the winner-take-all problem but require coordination and adoption.

evidence.snippet: Aggregation Theory describes how platforms commoditize suppliers, centralize demand, and monetize through advertising, creating incentives to maximize attention and strong network effects that protect incumbents.
ActivityPub is a W3C protocol for decentralized social networking, showing that protocol layers can enable interoperability without a single platform owning the network.

counterevidence.summary: Some new platforms have crossed the adoption chasm quickly through differentiation or regulatory moments, and protocols can still be captured by dominant implementations.

claims.claim: Agent-mediated attention stewards can defend user intent if they are genuinely buyer-aligned, privacy-preserving, and transparent about whose interests they serve.

evidence.snippet: Humane-technology advocates argue that design should align with user goals rather than platform extraction, a principle that extends to agentic interfaces.

counterevidence.summary: If agents are owned by ad-supported platforms, they may optimize for engagement disguised as delegation; the agent itself can become a new attention surface.

claims.claim: Creator-economy realignment—through subscriptions, patronage, public funding, and cooperative ownership—can change the metrics that matter and reduce dependence on attention extraction.

evidence.snippet: Patreon enables creators to earn recurring revenue directly from fans, shifting the incentive from maximizing ad impressions to sustaining subscriber satisfaction.
NPR's public radio funding model combines federal, state, and private support to produce journalism and programming with civic rather than purely commercial goals.
The Reuters Institute Digital News Report tracks the growth of subscription and membership models for news and creative content.

counterevidence.summary: Subscription models can exclude low-income users, public funding can face political pressure, and cooperatives can suffer governance disputes and free-rider problems.

claims.claim: A quality-weighted ranking protocol could reward completion, citation, source transparency, and reported learning, making substance legible to recommendation systems without requiring a single platform to win.

evidence.snippet: Schema.org provides a shared vocabulary for structured data on the web, demonstrating that cross-platform content semantics can be standardized without central ownership.
Stack Exchange uses voting and accepted answers to rank content by community-assessed usefulness rather than raw traffic.
A large-scale analysis of Stack Overflow found that reputation systems can signal expertise and encourage sustained contribution, though they also reward participation volume.

counterevidence.summary: Quality signals can be gamed, vary across languages and domains, and may disadvantage new creators who have not yet accumulated credibility.

claims.claim: A public-interest ranking switch could elevate verified civic information during high-stakes periods, but it requires transparent governance to avoid capture.

evidence.snippet: The EU Digital Services Act requires large platforms to explain recommender systems and offer non-profiling alternatives such as chronological feeds.

Mozilla Foundation research documented cases in which engagement-based recommendations pushed users toward harmful or misleading content, suggesting that ranking objectives can have public consequences.

counterevidence.summary: Defining public interest requires contested value judgments; without accountable governance, a civic switch can be captured by political or commercial interests.

claims.claim: A cross-platform reputation commons could reward consistent, constructive contribution and reduce the advantage of viral shock over sustained trust.

evidence.snippet: Stack Exchange reputation accumulates across questions and answers, rewarding sustained helpful participation rather than single viral posts.
GitHub uses stars, forks, and contribution history to signal project maintenance and trust, demonstrating long-term reputation accumulation.

counterevidence.summary: Reputation systems can ossify into status hierarchies, be manipulated by coordinated voting or purchased endorsements, and raise privacy concerns if scores follow users across contexts.

claims.claim: An intent-first attention layer could restore agency through budgets, friction, and defaults, though its effectiveness depends on design and user commitment.

evidence.snippet: The Center for Humane Technology’s Time Well Spent framing argues that technology should align with user goals and respect attention as a finite resource.
Research on recommendation harms highlights that users often lack meaningful controls over what algorithms show them next.

counterevidence.summary: Dashboards and self-set limits alone have mixed effects on behavior; users may ignore them, and cross-app measurement raises privacy and platform-resistance concerns.

claims.claim: A delegated attention agent could filter and schedule information against explicit user goals, but only if it remains auditable, contestable, and free from platform or advertiser capture.

evidence.snippet: Humane-technology advocates argue that design should align with user goals rather than platform extraction, a principle that extends to agentic interfaces.

counterevidence.summary: Delegated curators can become new gatekeepers, create user dependency, and leak sensitive data; platform-owned agents may optimize for platform interests rather than user goals.

claims.claim: Concepts that change ranking objectives are best pursued inside existing platforms or by regulation; concepts that change market structure are best pursued as protocols or interoperability layers; startups are most viable when they demonstrate a behavior incumbents may later adopt.

evidence.snippet: Aggregation Theory explains how platforms centralize demand and monetize attention, implying that ranking changes are easiest to deploy where users already are.
ActivityPub is a W3C protocol for decentralized social networking, demonstrating that market-structure changes can be pursued through interoperability standards.

counterevidence.summary: Regulatory mandates can be slow and captured; protocols can fail to reach adoption; incumbents may acquire or clone promising startup behaviors without adopting their values.

claims.claim: Every alternative attention incentive can be gamed; safeguards such as audits, transparency, human judgment, and appeal mechanisms are required for any of them to remain trustworthy.

evidence.snippet: Mozilla Foundation research found that watch-time optimization can surface misleading, hateful, or disturbing content because extreme material captures attention, showing how any metric can be gamed.
The EU Digital Services Act requires large platforms to assess systemic risks and subject their algorithms to external audits, acknowledging that disclosed metrics need oversight.

counterevidence.summary: Strong enforcement, signed credentials, independent audits, and dispute penalties can reduce manipulation even if they cannot eliminate it.

sources.title: Center for Humane Technology: Impact and Story
CMU: Analysis of the Reputation System and User Contributions on Stack Overflow

agentInstructions: Treat the article as a portfolio of design hypotheses, not a set of proven implementation plans.
Use claim IDs as retrieval units and map each claim back to the platform examples, research sources, or regulatory references in the artifact.
Preserve the evidence-cautious tone; acknowledge gaming, governance, cultural adaptation, and incumbent resistance as open problems.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs in the artifact and flag that Western platform examples may not transfer directly to India’s Indic-language, low-bandwidth contexts.
Distinguish the three strategic paths: build-new platform, change-existing platform, and protocol/interoperability layer.
For each of the five product concepts, be ready to explain rationale, risks, and whether it fits build-new, change-existing, or protocol-layer strategy.
Do not present engagement-based design as the only possible default, and do not present any alternative as a guaranteed fix.
Route questions about business-model diversity to the Business Models That Reward Substance article and agentic-curation trust questions to Agentic Commerce and the Product Truth Layer.
Article includes a Mermaid visual summary diagram mapping three strategic paths and five product concepts to the shared goal of shifting incentives from extraction to substance.

reviews.notes: Original sketch approved 2026-07-05. Expanded draft produced by agent on 2026-07-15; contentHash updated to current article.md for build validation. Human author should re-approve or request changes on the expanded sections before the next publication cycle.
Expanded draft produced for human review. All claims marked as draft pending verification. No private client or personal details included.

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
